learning_objectives: Identify the key elements of effective public speaking
Understand the psychology of speech anxiety and strategies for managing it
Analyze what makes a speech memorable
readings: The Art of Public Speaking Ch. 1-2 by Stephen Lucas

lecture_notes.introduction: Public speaking is consistently ranked among the most feared activities, yet it is one of the most valuable skills in any career. This course transforms anxiety into competence through practice, feedback, and technique.
key_concepts: The communication apprehension cycle and how to break it
Ethos, pathos, and logos in speech
Audience analysis: demographics, psychographics, and context
The myth of the natural speaker: skill vs. talent
Ethical responsibilities of public speakers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Watch and analyze two TED talks: one polished, one with visible nervousness. Discuss how each handles audience engagement.

review.value: Fear triggers avoidance, avoidance prevents practice, lack of practice maintains fear. Breaking the cycle requires deliberate, repeated exposure.
Before writing a speech, identify who will hear it, what they already know, what they care about, and what you want them to do

learning_objectives: Structure a speech with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion
Apply organizational patterns: chronological, topical, problem-solution, cause-effect
Craft attention-getting openings and memorable closings
readings: The Art of Public Speaking Ch. 8-9
Talk Like TED Ch. 3 by Carmine Gallo

lecture_notes.introduction: Structure is what separates a speech from a ramble. A well-organized speech is easier to deliver, easier to follow, and easier to remember.
key_concepts: The rule of three: audiences retain three main points best
Organizational patterns and when to use each
Attention-getting devices: story, question, statistic, quotation
Transitions and signposts between main points
The call to action: ending with purpose
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students outline a 3-minute speech using a provided topic and organizational pattern card

review.value: Three main points is optimal. Two feels incomplete, four feels overwhelming. Each point should be roughly equal in weight and development.
Internal summaries ('Now that we've seen X...') and previews ('Next, let's examine Y...') guide the audience through your structure

class_schedule.topic: Delivery Techniques: Voice, Body, and Presence
learning_objectives: Control vocal variety: pace, pitch, volume, and pauses
Use body language, gestures, and eye contact effectively
Manage physical space and stage presence
readings: The Art of Public Speaking Ch. 12-13

lecture_notes.introduction: Content is only half of a speech. Delivery determines whether your audience actually hears and retains your message. Today we focus on the physical and vocal tools that bring words to life.
key_concepts: Vocal variety: the antidote to monotone delivery
The power of the pause: strategic silence
Eye contact patterns for groups of different sizes
Purposeful gestures vs. nervous fidgeting
Power posing and pre-speech body preparation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student delivers the same 30-second passage, first in monotone, then with vocal variety. Class provides feedback.

review.value: Vary pace (slow for emphasis, faster for energy), pitch (higher for excitement, lower for gravity), volume (louder for urgency, softer to draw in)
Pausing before a key point builds anticipation; pausing after lets it sink in. Three seconds feels long to the speaker but perfect to the audience.

learning_objectives: Design presentation slides that support rather than replace the speaker
Apply the principles of effective visual aid design
readings: Presentation Zen Ch. 4-6 by Garr Reynolds
Slide:ology selected chapters by Nancy Duarte

lecture_notes.introduction: Slides should be a visual aid, not a teleprompter. The best presentations use slides to reinforce, illustrate, and emotionally connect, while the speaker provides the substance.
key_concepts: The assertion-evidence slide model
One idea per slide principle
Font size, contrast, and readability at distance
Images over bullet points: picture superiority effect
The Takahashi method and other minimalist approaches
lecture_notes.demonstration: Before-and-after slide makeover: transform a text-heavy slide deck into a visual presentation
setup_requirements: PowerPoint, Keynote, or Google Slides access
Sample bad slides for makeover exercise (provided)

review.value: Slide headline states a complete assertion (not a topic label); the body provides visual evidence supporting that assertion
People remember 65% of information presented with images after 3 days vs. 10% from text alone

learning_objectives: Construct a persuasive speech using Monroe's Motivated Sequence
Use evidence and emotional appeal ethically in persuasion
Anticipate and address counterarguments in a speech
readings: The Art of Public Speaking Ch. 16-17
Thank You for Arguing Ch. 2-4 by Jay Heinrichs

lecture_notes.introduction: Persuasive speaking is where all your skills converge: research, structure, delivery, and audience awareness. The goal is not manipulation but making a compelling case that moves people to think or act differently.
key_concepts: Monroe's Motivated Sequence: attention, need, satisfaction, visualization, action
Balancing logos, ethos, and pathos
Inoculation theory: addressing counterarguments preemptively
Ethical persuasion vs. manipulation
The persuasive power of personal stories and concrete examples
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students outline a persuasive speech using Monroe's Motivated Sequence on a campus issue
setup_requirements: List of campus policy topics for selection

review.value: Attention (hook), Need (establish the problem), Satisfaction (present the solution), Visualization (show the outcome), Action (tell audience what to do)
Raising and refuting counterarguments in advance makes your audience resistant to opposing messages, like a vaccine against counter-persuasion

learning_objectives: Apply frameworks for organizing thoughts quickly under pressure
Handle hostile or unexpected questions with composure
Practice impromptu speaking in low-stakes settings
readings: The Art of Public Speaking Ch. 14
Thinking on Your Feet by Marian Woodall, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Not every speaking situation is planned. Job interviews, meetings, and Q&A sessions require the ability to organize thoughts and speak clearly with little or no preparation.
key_concepts: PREP framework: Point, Reason, Example, Point
The bridge technique: redirecting from a difficult question to your message
Buying time without saying 'um': restate, pause, clarify
Handling hostile questions: acknowledge, reframe, respond
Practice methods for improving impromptu skills
lecture_notes.demonstration: Impromptu speaking rounds: each student draws a topic card and delivers a 60-second PREP response

review.value: State your Point, give a Reason, offer an Example, restate your Point. This creates a coherent answer in under 60 seconds.
Acknowledge the question, then pivot: 'That's an important concern, and it connects to the larger issue of...' This lets you address the question while steering toward your key message.

questions.question: Describe three different organizational patterns for speeches and explain a specific situation where each would be most effective.
questions.answer: Chronological: organizes by time sequence. Best for a speech about the history of the internet or steps in a process. Problem-solution: presents a problem then proposes a solution. Best for persuasive speeches like arguing for renewable energy policy. Cause-effect: explains why something happens and its consequences. Best for informative speeches like explaining how sleep deprivation affects academic performance.

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'vocal variety' and identify four specific vocal techniques a speaker can use to keep an audience engaged.
questions.answer: Vocal variety means intentionally varying how you use your voice to maintain audience interest and emphasize key points. Four techniques: (1) Pace variation: slow down for important points, speed up for exciting content. (2) Volume changes: speak louder for urgency, softer to draw the audience in. (3) Pitch modulation: vary high and low tones to avoid monotone delivery. (4) Strategic pauses: silence before or after a key point creates emphasis and gives the audience time to process.

questions.question: What is the assertion-evidence slide model and how does it differ from traditional bullet-point slides? Why is it more effective?
questions.answer: The assertion-evidence model uses a complete sentence as the slide headline that states a specific claim, supported by a visual image or diagram as evidence. Traditional slides use topic labels as headlines ('Market Trends') with bullet points listing details. The assertion-evidence model is more effective because the audience immediately understands the point being made, the visual evidence is more memorable than text bullets (picture superiority effect), and it forces the speaker to clarify their argument rather than hiding behind vague topic labels.

rubric.key: Explanation of why it is more effective

questions.question: List the five steps of Monroe's Motivated Sequence and write a one-sentence example for each step in a speech advocating for a campus recycling program.
questions.answer: 1. Attention: 'Every day, our campus sends 2,000 pounds of recyclable material straight to the landfill.' 2. Need: 'Without a recycling program, we are wasting resources and contributing to environmental damage that affects our own community.' 3. Satisfaction: 'A campus-wide recycling program with sorted bins at every building entrance would divert 80% of this waste.' 4. Visualization: 'Imagine walking across a campus known for environmental leadership, where prospective students choose us because of our sustainability commitment.' 5. Action: 'Sign the petition at the student union this week to bring this program to a vote at the next student government meeting.'

rubric.key: All five steps correctly named in order
Each example sentence matches its step
Examples form a coherent persuasive flow

major_assignments.title: Persuasive Speech with Visual Aids
major_assignments.description: Prepare and deliver a 6-8 minute persuasive speech on a topic of your choice using Monroe's Motivated Sequence. The speech must be supported by well-designed visual aids and include at least three credible sources cited verbally during delivery.
objectives: Demonstrate persuasive speech organization using Monroe's Motivated Sequence
Apply effective delivery techniques: vocal variety, eye contact, purposeful gestures
Design and use visual aids that enhance rather than distract from the message
Integrate evidence from credible sources into oral presentation
deliverables: Full-sentence speech outline submitted before delivery
Works cited page with at least 3 credible sources
In-class delivery (6-8 minutes, recorded for self-review)
Self-evaluation form completed after reviewing recording

criteria: Clear thesis and persuasive purpose
Monroe's Motivated Sequence followed completely
Evidence from credible sources integrated naturally
Counterarguments addressed effectively
Vocal variety: pace, pitch, volume, and pauses used effectively
Eye contact maintained with audience throughout
Purposeful gestures and confident body language
Time within 6-8 minute range
Slides support rather than replace the speaker
Clean design with readable text and relevant images
Smooth integration of visuals into delivery
No reading from slides
Honest assessment of strengths and weaknesses
Specific observations from video review
Actionable improvement goals identified
Demonstrates understanding of delivery concepts
Compelling persuasive speech that moves the audience to action
Confident, polished delivery demonstrating mastery of vocal and physical techniques
Visual aids that enhance the message with professional design
Thoughtful self-evaluation demonstrating growth mindset

speeches.description: Three graded speeches of increasing complexity: informative, persuasive, and special occasion

quizzes.description: Assessment of public speaking concepts and techniques

impromptu_exercises.description: Regular impromptu speaking practice scored on organization and composure

peer_evaluations.description: Quality of written and oral feedback provided to classmates on their speeches

self_evaluations.description: Thoughtful self-assessment using video review of own performances

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all speech days, both to deliver and to serve as audience. Missing a speech day without prior arrangement results in a zero for that speech.
course_policies.late_submissions: Outlines and written materials are due before the speech. Late outlines result in a 10% deduction on the speech grade. Speeches cannot be made up after the assigned day without documented emergency.
course_policies.collaboration: Speeches must be original work. Peer feedback is encouraged during practice sessions. Using someone else's speech or purchasing a speech is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All sources must be cited verbally during the speech and in the written outline. Fabricating sources or statistics is grounds for course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to presentation software and a smartphone or camera for recording practice sessions. The classroom provides a projector and audio system.
